summary: The manuscript's dominant redundancy problem is proof-of-baseline accumulation. The phrase 'eleven years' appears 45 times across 16 of 18 chapters, re-proving Maren's fraudulent history long past establishment. Thessa's 'stained hands' descriptor appears 9+ times across 4 chapters with a cluster of 4 in Ch14 bleeding into the adjacent Ch15 opening. Aldric's 'order that no longer existed' formula repeats 6 times verbatim across 6 chapters. Vess's 'flat' voice tag appears 12+ times across 8 chapters. Adjacent-chapter redundancy is concentrated in Ch4-Ch5 (Derry behavioral tells), Ch7-Ch8 (Thessa clinical voice, Derry mended tear, Maren fraud premise), Ch8-Ch9 (Vess flat voice, eleven years), and Ch14-Ch15 (Thessa stained hands). Setting re-descriptions are clean; recurring locations enter through change rather than re-inventory. Em-dash density is 24.6% manuscript-wide (582/2362 prose lines), far exceeding the 10% threshold, with extreme bimodal distribution: three chapters at 0% and five chapters above 36%. Eight consistency contradictions were found, the most severe being a tenfold bounty-amount discrepancy between Ch3 (4,000 gold crowns) and Ch4 (400 gold), and a Ch14-to-Ch15 character-attribute migration where Kael's gender, root-chewing habit, and Errin's name and clay jars all shift between adjacent chapters.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 line 9 establishes the fraud ('pretending to eat sins'); by Ch5 the phrase 'eleven years' has appeared 20+ times in Chs 1-4. Ch5 lines 950 and 954 restate it in consecutive paragraphs ('the thing he'd been pretending to do for eleven years'; 'For eleven years, every ritual Maren had performed was about closing'), and line 966 adds 'a thousand fake rituals' restating the same point a third time within 16 lines.
redundancy_findings.problem: Maren's fraudulent history is re-proved three times in a dense exposition block (lines 950-966) without materially new information. The reader has encountered this baseline 20+ times already. First established Ch1 line 9. Manuscript total: 45 occurrences of 'eleven years' across 16 chapters.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep ONE of the three restatements in the consumption-attempt passage (line 950 is strongest because it transitions into action). Cut the expository paragraph at line 954 entirely — the reader knows this. Rephrase line 966 to focus on the muscle-memory reflex without re-explaining that the rituals were fake.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The passage from lines 950-970 contains at most one reference to the duration or fraudulence of Maren's past rituals. The remaining text focuses on the present-tense attempt and its physical consequences.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 line 1916: 'faking his way across Fenhold's farmland'; line 1928: 'Eleven years of contamination in named villages, children growing up in the residue of quests he'd only pretended to clear'; line 1952: 'Eleven years of performing a System role'; line 1926: 'The difference was that the System-sighted saw it with data overlaid — quest details, threat levels, completion percentages — and Maren saw the raw thing.' System-blindness formula near-identical to Ch4 line 868. Adjacent to Ch7 which contains 5 'eleven years' uses.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 opening bundles three redundant baselines: (1) the fraud premise at line 1916, (2) 'eleven years' twice at lines 1928 and 1952, and (3) the System-blindness perceptual formula at line 1926 restating the 'everyone sees data, Maren sees raw light' contrast established at Ch4 line 868. All three are well-established by Ch8.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Line 1916: replace the fraud-recap clause with a sensory detail about the road. Line 1926: cut the explanatory contrast ('The difference was that the System-sighted saw it with data overlaid...'); by Ch8 the reader understands Maren's blindness. Line 1928: keep the guilt-weight but cut 'Eleven years of contamination in named villages' — replace with a specific image. Line 1952: cut 'Eleven years of performing a System role.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 8's opening three pages contain zero uses of the phrase 'eleven years' and no expository explanation of how System-sighted perception differs from Maren's.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 line 2343: 'he said it the way he'd said a thousand things over eleven years of fraud'; line 2349: 'The lie was smooth enough after eleven years.' Adjacent to Ch8 (2 uses at lines 1928, 1952).
redundancy_findings.problem: The fraud-duration baseline is re-proved twice more in Ch9 narration (lines 2343, 2349), immediately adjacent to Ch8 which also restates it twice. By Ch9, the manuscript has used 'eleven years' 35+ times.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Line 2343: keep the lie-delivery action but cut 'over eleven years of fraud.' Line 2349: cut 'after eleven years' entirely; the smoothness of the lie is self-evident from context.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9 contains zero instances of 'eleven years' in narrator exposition. The market-deception scene conveys Maren's skill through action.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 line 3102: 'Same chipped rim. Same market glaze. Same stupid prop he'd used for eleven years.' By Ch12 the phrase has appeared 39 times in prior chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: The bowl-as-prop baseline and eleven-year duration are re-proved in Ch12 narration despite 39 prior uses. First established Ch1 line 9; restated in Ch2 line 388, Ch5 line 966, and elsewhere.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut 'he'd used for eleven years' from the bowl description. The 'chipped rim' and 'market glaze' already identify the bowl. If the fraud context is needed, replace with a forward-looking beat.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The bowl reference at line 3102 does not restate its duration of use or its status as a prop.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 line 1605: 'a real one, the kind who actually cleared contamination instead of performing clearance while the contamination sat untouched in the soil underneath...someone who'd spent a lifetime doing what Maren had spent a lifetime pretending to do.' First established Ch1 lines 9-33.
redundancy_findings.problem: The narrator restates the real-vs-fake sin-eater premise as a full paragraph (line 1605) in Ch7, as if the reader needs reminding of the novel's central conceit by Chapter 7 of 18. The Thessa dialogue lines (1695, 1713, 1829) earn their restatements through dramatic confrontation; the narration at 1605 does not.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or compress line 1605 to a single clause. Replace the explanatory aside with Maren's emotional state or a sensory detail from the market approach. Let Thessa's dialogue do the work of contrasting real and fake.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The passage between lines 1603-1607 does not contain an expository explanation of what a real sin-eater does versus what Maren does.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 line 3572: 'a ceramic bowl in her stained hands'; line 3652: 'stained dark, edges worn smooth, a tool that had seen thirty years of genuine use'; line 3724: 'Thessa's stained hands'; line 3596: Kael's hands described via comparison to Thessa's staining. First established Ch7 lines 1637, 1657, 1673, 1777.
redundancy_findings.problem: Thessa's stained hands are mentioned 4 times within Ch14 (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724) plus 'thirty years' at line 3652. The trait was established 5 times in Ch7. Total: 9+ instances of this single physical descriptor.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep ONE stained-hands reference in Ch14 (line 3572 on first reappearance is strongest). Cut staining from lines 3652 and 3724. At line 3596, describe Kael's hands independently. Cut 'thirty years' from line 3652.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 14 contains at most one reference to Thessa's stained hands and zero uses of 'thirty years' in connection to Thessa's experience.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 line 3901: 'her stained hands were empty.' Adjacent to Ch14 which mentions stained hands 4 times (lines 3572, 3596, 3652, 3724).
redundancy_findings.problem: Thessa's stained hands re-described at Ch15 opening (line 3901), immediately adjacent to Ch14's 4-mention cluster. First established Ch7 line 1637.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace 'her stained hands were empty' with a different physical detail conveying the same information (she put something down in a hurry). Use posture, expression, or movement instead of staining.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15's opening Thessa description does not use the word 'stained' or reference hand discoloration.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch18 line 4648: 'Her hands, stained dark from thirty years of ritual contamination exposure, made the fruit look very white.' Ch7 line 1679: 'hers dark with thirty years of absorbed contamination'; Ch14 line 3652: 'thirty years of genuine use.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The 'stained hands + thirty years' combination appears for the third time at Ch18 line 4648. The full descriptor package was established in Ch7 line 1679 and restated in Ch14 line 3652.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the visual contrast ('made the fruit look very white') but cut 'from thirty years of ritual contamination exposure.' The word 'stained' alone or 'her dark hands' is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Line 4648 does not contain the phrase 'thirty years.' Thessa's hands may be referenced without restating career duration.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16 line 4308: 'A report delivered to an order that no longer existed.' Also at Ch2 line 552 (first use), Ch4 line 848, Ch9 line 2461, Ch14 line 3546, Ch17 line 4483. Total: 6 uses of the exact formula across 6 chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: Aldric's 'reports filed to an order that no longer existed' is a fixed formula used 6 times verbatim. The Ch17 use (line 4483) functions as deliberate dissolution of the motif; the Ch16 use at line 4308 is a plain restatement that dulls the Ch17 payoff.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 4308, cut the 'to an order that no longer existed' formula. Convey Aldric's formality with fresh language. Reserve the formula's final appearance for Ch17 line 4483 where it is deliberately broken.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 16 does not contain the phrase 'order that no longer existed' or any close synonym.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 line 2421: 'The word was flat, professional, a field assessment delivered without emphasis.' Adjacent to Ch8 lines 1980 ('flat, precise, billing by the syllable'), 1994 ('flat as ever'), 2118 ('Vess's flat delivery'). Total: 12+ uses of 'flat' for Vess across 8 chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: Vess's voice is tagged as 'flat' in nearly every chapter she appears in (12+ uses across Ch4, Ch8, Ch9, Ch10, Ch14, Ch16, Ch17). The adjacent Ch8-Ch9 cluster (3+1 uses) is the most jarring. First established Ch4 line 1046. The pattern blunts the payoff when her flatness breaks in Ch15.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 2421, convey Vess's urgency through speech content and brevity rather than re-tagging her voice. Cut the 'flat, professional' descriptor. More broadly, limit 'flat' as a Vess tag to 4-5 total uses to preserve impact.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9 does not describe Vess's voice as 'flat' or 'professional.' Her style is conveyed through speech content and action.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 line 872: 'He talked as he always did when he was scared: too much and about nothing, each sentence hitched to the next.' Ch3 line 589: 'the leek monologue was a tell — the worse things got, the more Derry talked...The content didn't matter. What mattered was the noise, the filling of space.' Adjacent chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: Derry's talking-when-scared behavioral tell is described in full expository form in both Ch3 (line 589) and Ch4 (line 872), adjacent chapters. Both explain the same mechanism: Derry fills silence with chatter when frightened.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 872, cut the explanatory aside 'as he always did when he was scared: too much and about nothing...' Show Derry talking — the reader will recognize the pattern from Ch3. Line 874 ('Maren let him talk. It was familiar.') already provides sufficient recognition.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Line 872 presents Derry's nervous chatter through action or dialogue, not through narrator explanation of the behavioral pattern.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8 line 1930: 'Thessa had said it the way she said everything — clinically, as if the fact of his approaching dissolution was a drainage problem and she was describing the pipe.' Ch7 line 1661: 'clipped, clinical — a surgeon cataloguing damage.' Adjacent chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: Thessa's clinical voice is re-described in Ch8 (line 1930) with a medical/infrastructure simile, adjacent to Ch7 where it was first established with a parallel simile at line 1661. Both describe the same trait: Thessa delivers devastating information in detached professional register.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1930, cut 'the way she said everything — clinically, as if...' and let the quoted content convey Thessa's delivery. The reader met Thessa one chapter ago.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Line 1930 does not re-characterize Thessa's delivery style through a clinical/medical/infrastructure simile.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch5 lines 1387, 1407, 1417 describe Maren's hand tremor and the 'pressing palms flat against thighs' management gesture. Ch6 lines 1430, 1438 restate both the tremor and the same pressing gesture within the first 20 lines, and lines 1526, 1562, 1564, 1582 continue describing the same tremor throughout. Total: 8+ mentions of the same tremor across Ch5-Ch6 (~200 lines).
redundancy_findings.problem: Maren's post-fight hand tremor is established in Ch5 and then re-proved 5+ times in adjacent Ch6 without new information. The 'pressing palms flat against thighs' gesture appears at Ch5 lines 1387, 1407, 1417 and Ch6 line 1430 — four times in two adjacent chapters describing the identical coping mechanism.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch6, keep ONE tremor reference early (line 1430 or 1438) and cut subsequent restatements. The pressing-palms gesture should not reappear in Ch6 after appearing three times in Ch5. Let the tremor be present through action (dropped objects, difficulty with tasks) rather than repeated narrator inventory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 6 contains at most one explicit mention of hand tremor or shaking. The pressing-palms-flat gesture does not appear in Ch6.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 65 of 162 prose lines (40.1%) in Ch9 contain em-dashes. Manuscript-wide density: 582/2362 prose lines = 24.6%, exceeding the 10% threshold by 2.5x. Ch9 is the densest chapter.
redundancy_findings.problem: Em-dash density in Ch9 is 40.1% (65/162 prose lines), the highest in the manuscript. The overall manuscript density of 24.6% must be reduced below 8% (target: ~189 em-dash lines total, requiring cuts of ~393). Ch9's 65 em-dash lines are the single largest contributor.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch9's em-dash count from 65 to approximately 13 (targeting 8% chapter density). Convert parenthetical em-dash pairs to commas, colons, or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for genuine interruptions and dramatic pivots. Prioritize cutting em-dashes in exposition and description; preserve them in dialogue and action beats.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9 contains em-dashes on no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 24 of 162 lines).

redundancy_findings.evidence: 48 of 121 prose lines (39.7%) in Ch17 contain em-dashes. Manuscript-wide: 582/2362 = 24.6%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Em-dash density in Ch17 is 39.7% (48/121 prose lines), the second densest chapter. Contributing 48 lines to the manuscript-wide excess.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch17's em-dash count from 48 to approximately 10 (targeting 8%). Convert parenthetical em-dash pairs to commas or separate sentences. The Woundmouth climax merits some retained em-dashes for rhythm; cut them from exposition and System-description passages.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 17 contains em-dashes on no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 18 of 121 lines).

redundancy_findings.evidence: 62 of 169 prose lines (36.7%) in Ch4 contain em-dashes. Manuscript-wide: 582/2362 = 24.6%. Notable: three chapters (Ch5, Ch10, Ch12) contain zero em-dashes, creating an extreme bimodal distribution.
redundancy_findings.problem: Em-dash density in Ch4 is 36.7% (62/169 prose lines), the third highest by absolute count. The manuscript shows extreme stylistic inconsistency: 5 chapters exceed 35% while 3 chapters have 0%, suggesting different drafting passes with different punctuation habits.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch4's em-dash count from 62 to approximately 14 (targeting 8%). Convert parenthetical asides to commas or periods. The bimodal distribution across the manuscript also suggests a global normalization pass is needed to bring all chapters into the 5-10% range.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 4 contains em-dashes on no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 25 of 169 lines).

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch3 lines 717, 723, 733, 763, 793: bounty stated as 'four thousand gold crowns' five times. Ch4 lines 906, 1092, 1172: bounty stated as 'four hundred gold' three times. Continuity sheet confirms canonical amount is 4,000 gold crowns.
consistency_findings.problem: The bounty on Maren drops by a factor of ten between Ch3 and Ch4. Ch3 consistently states 'four thousand gold crowns' (5 uses) while Ch4 consistently states 'four hundred gold' (3 uses). The continuity sheet confirms 4,000 as canonical. This is a tenfold magnitude error that appears in dialogue, narration, and System readout.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change all three Ch4 bounty references (lines 906, 1092, 1172) from 'four hundred gold' to 'four thousand gold crowns' to match the canonical Ch3 figure and continuity sheet.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Every bounty amount in Chapter 4 reads 'four thousand gold crowns,' matching the Ch3 establishment and the continuity sheet.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 line 87: 'I am Aldric of the Order of the Veil. Knight-paladin of the Fourth Seal.' Ch4 line 1004: 'I am — I was — Aldric, Knight-Servitor of the Order of the—'. Continuity sheet confirms 'knight-paladin of the Order of the Veil's Fourth Seal.'
consistency_findings.problem: Aldric introduces himself as 'Knight-paladin of the Fourth Seal' in Ch1 (line 87) and as 'Knight-Servitor' in Ch4 (line 1004). These are different rank designations. The continuity sheet confirms 'knight-paladin' as canonical.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change 'Knight-Servitor' at line 1004 to 'Knight-paladin of the Fourth Seal' (or an appropriate truncation given the interrupted speech: 'Knight-paladin of the—').
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Line 1004 uses 'Knight-paladin' rather than 'Knight-Servitor.' Aldric's title is consistent with Ch1 line 87.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch14 lines 3596-3868: Kael is consistently 'he/his/him' ('The older man finished chewing his root' at 3596; 'Kael said' with 'he' pronouns at 3628, 3822; 'Kael spat his root' at 3868). Ch15 line 3881: 'Kael had just come out of the western shelter, still pulling her coat straight from sleep.'
consistency_findings.problem: Kael uses male pronouns throughout Ch14 (he/his/him across 7+ references) and switches to female pronoun ('her coat') at Ch15 line 3881 with no explanation. Adjacent chapters.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 3881, change 'her coat' to 'his coat' to match Ch14's consistent male pronoun usage for Kael.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: All pronoun references to Kael in Chapter 15 use 'he/his/him,' matching Chapter 14.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch14 lines 3628-3868: Kael is the root-chewer (lines 3628, 3676, 3822, 3868: 'Kael spat his root into the dirt'). Ch14 line 3868: Errin gathers clay jars ('Errin stooped to gather her clay jars'). Ch15 line 3879: Ruald is now the root-chewer ('Ruald sat on his upturned bucket... chewing root'); Petra has the clay jars ('Petra had the fire going and three clay jars'). Ch15 lines 3899, 3909, 3927, 3947: Ruald continues with root and dies with it (line 4059: 'Ruald with the root in his cheek'). Errin does not appear in Ch15; Denna and Ruald appear without introduction.
consistency_findings.problem: Between Ch14 and Ch15, two character-attribute swaps occur: (1) the root-chewing habit migrates from Kael to the new character Ruald, and (2) the clay jars migrate from Errin to Petra. Additionally, Errin (Ch14) disappears and Denna (Ch15) appears without introduction, suggesting a name change. The death summary at line 4059 follows Ch15's assignments, confirming the errors are in Ch15's reassignment rather than Ch14's establishment.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile the Ch14-Ch15 roster. Either: (A) restore root-chewing to Kael and jars to Errin in Ch15, making Ruald and Denna new characters who are introduced properly; or (B) revise Ch14 to match Ch15's roster if Ruald and Denna are the intended characters. Ensure Errin/Denna naming is consistent. The death summary (line 4059) should match whichever roster is canonical.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The root-chewing habit belongs to the same named character in both Ch14 and Ch15. The clay jars belong to the same named character in both chapters. No character appears in Ch15 without having been introduced in Ch14.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch3 line 583: matches for 'Pell wasn't three yet' or similar; Ch10 line 2688: 'Pell's going to be three next month'; Ch13 line 3381: 'She's turning three while we're on the road.' Ch6 line 1520: 'She's three. She's three years old and she has opinions about shoes.'
consistency_findings.problem: Pell's age contradicts between Ch6 and all other references. Ch3, Ch10, and Ch13 consistently say Pell is not yet three (approaching her third birthday). Ch6 line 1520 has Derry state 'She's three' flatly, contradicting the other chapters.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1520, change 'She's three. She's three years old' to 'She's almost three. She's not even three years old' or similar phrasing consistent with Pell approaching but not having reached her third birthday.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Line 1520 does not state that Pell is three years old. Her age reference is consistent with Ch10 line 2688 ('going to be three next month').

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 line 9: 'purchased from a market stall in Fenhold for four coppers when Maren was seventeen.' Ch16 line 4222: 'Three coppers. The woman had tried to sell him a nicer one for five, and he'd said no, because he was seventeen and two coppers was dinner.' Continuity sheet: 'bought at seventeen for four coppers.'
consistency_findings.problem: The bowl's purchase price is stated as four coppers in Ch1 (line 9) and the continuity sheet, but three coppers in Ch16 (line 4222). The Ch16 version includes arithmetic (3 + 2 = 5) that supports the three-copper figure internally, but contradicts the established canonical price.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 4222, change 'Three coppers' to 'Four coppers' and adjust the arithmetic: 'Four coppers. The woman had tried to sell him a nicer one for six, and he'd said no, because he was seventeen and two coppers was dinner.' Or match to Ch1's canonical four-copper price with appropriate arithmetic.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The bowl's purchase price in Ch16 matches Ch1's 'four coppers.' Any surrounding arithmetic is internally consistent.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch3 line 811: 'a second pair of boots strapped to the outside' of Derry's bag. Ch6 line 1510: 'Derry was lacing his boots back up.' Ch9 lines 2367-2369: '"You're wearing sandals." ... He was, in fact, wearing sandals. He'd been wearing sandals for the entire trip.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch9 states Derry has been wearing sandals 'for the entire trip' (line 2369), contradicting Ch3 where he packs boots (line 811) and Ch6 where he is lacing boots (line 1510). These are not reconcilable — if he had boots in Ch3 and Ch6, he was not wearing sandals for the entire trip.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 2369, remove 'He'd been wearing sandals for the entire trip.' Replace with phrasing that acknowledges his footwear without contradicting earlier chapters — e.g., he switched to sandals recently, or he packed boots but changed at some point. Alternatively, revise Ch3 and Ch6 to show sandals instead of boots if sandals are the intended canonical footwear.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Derry's footwear references in Ch9 do not claim he has worn sandals for the entire trip. His footwear history is consistent with Ch3 (boots packed) and Ch6 (boots laced).

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 line 15: 'his twelve-copper fee'; Ch2 line 464: 'collected twelve coppers.' Ch7 line 1901: 'Maren and Derry split fifteen coppers and went to the inn.' Continuity sheet: 'About 12 coppers per rite.'
consistency_findings.problem: The ritual fee is established as twelve coppers in Ch1 (line 15), Ch2 (line 464), and the continuity sheet. Ch7 line 1901 states 'split fifteen coppers,' which contradicts the established fee. If the standard fee was twelve coppers, they cannot split fifteen from a single ritual.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1901, change 'fifteen coppers' to 'twelve coppers' to match the established fee. The sentence reads naturally either way.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Line 1901 states 'twelve coppers' rather than 'fifteen coppers,' matching Ch1 and the continuity sheet.
